sentences.text: In September.
In September of '42, 1942.
And we were there for about four weeks.
When we heard a rumor-- because after the liquidation in our home town in Poland, there  were still some few Jewish-- some Jewish population left.
And my two sisters that were hidden up in the farm, they went back to our hometown.
And we kept in contact.

So when we heard in camp that was a rumor that they go and liquidate the whole town and liquidate everybody-- and a neighbor that had a home someplace and they had some valuables hidden in the attic-- and they thought if I would be there I could go and find it and maybe send it to them to the camp.
They were in a different camp.
And that's when I went away from the camp-- was able to run away.
It wasn't that bad.
And it was not far from-- from the camp that we were.
There was a Polish family.
And I told them if he would go with me, like buy me a ticket on the train to go to Poland-- if we-- he would get there, I would give him things from the house because what we have.
So sure enough, him and, I think, one of his daughters and he bought a ticket for them and also for me--
For the two-- for this-- for the Polish-- for him and his daughter and myself.
No.
And we came to our hometown in 4 o'clock-- whatever, early in the morning and I knocked on the house and the door.
And my sisters, they were surprised to see me.
And I did find something in the attic.
I don't remember whether it was money or gold pieces or something like that.
And we were able to turn it into some money.
We sent some to the-- to the neighbor.
I was able to keep some.
I found more than-- than we knew.
And even then, you know, the last minute before the night before I-- the second liquidation, I don't know whether a German think they-- they caught us doing something.
And we almost got shot that night, you know, with a pistol to my head.
Yeah.
Yeah, in the town.
Well, it was so fast.
I mean, we-- like I said, anything you did, you weren't supposed to do, whatever you did.
And for some reason, I-- we had some things and we were able to give them, like bribing-- let us go.
And sure enough the following morning was like the liquidation.
And that evening, which was, I don't know, 10 o'clock at night or something, my two sisters, myself, and a cousin of ours ran away and we went to that farm, which was about seven or eight kilometers.
It was so pitch dark that night that you talked to each other, like seeing-- but you couldn't see each other.
And ?] [? they were like-- ?] You had to hold hands, otherwise you didn't know where-- you would've got lost.
To that place it was about seven kilometers.
And we did get there.
I remember my two sisters and myself, we wind up at that farm.
My cousin went to a different place.
And the farmer kept us for about a week or 10 days.
And then he said he's afraid he wouldn't want to keep us any longer.
What we planned to do that he can keep us?
So I told my sisters, let's try to go to get to Krakow to Prokocim in that labor camp and maybe we-- because-- stay there and see what happened.
So he promised-- you know, we didn't know how to go to Krakow.
We couldn't go on the highway.
We just went through dirt roads and back-- back roads and-- and things like that.
So one of this sons was going to go ahead of us a mile or a mile and a half.
And that was in November.
There was snow, was cold, was dirt.
And when he went about a mile from the farm, something like this, though we followed him until no direction to go, and then he disappeared.
And my sister-- one of my sisters, you know-- What's her name?
Helen.
Helen.
She started to cry.
And I said, don't worry.
We'll make it.
We'll make it.
And somehow we did.
We didn't get quite to Krakow.
We wound up maybe-- in a-- also in a-- at a Polish-- in a farmhouse.
And they recognized us that we were Jewish.
But this family, for some reason, they were nice to us.
They let us stay overnight in-- in a barn.
And the next day, the-- one of the-- their son worked in Krakow.
And it was close.
And we took, I think, a street car.
And we went into Krakow with them, with him-- not like we were with him, but in the same place and wouldn't be recognized.
And when we got to Krakow, there was the ghetto in Krakow.
So through a-- the-- it wasn't-- the ghetto wasn't that guarded that you couldn't get in.
It was more guarded getting out.
And we got into the ghetto.
And I don't remember whether my sisters had a place where to stay or not to stay.
My two sisters.
Helen and Sabina.
Yeah.
And I left them in ghetto.
And I went to-- back to camp because my brothers--
Prokocim.
We went back to Prokocim, which it also was about five-- five kilometers or something like that.
Yeah.
From-- from the ghetto.
Yeah.
And I went back to camp, and I found my two brothers.
It was Machel and David.
And I told them what happened, where we were at everything.
And they were trying to-- and they were able to get my sisters into that camp.
And what they were doing, they opened like a kitchen.
And they were cooking.
And they were-- like-- it was almost like a private enterprise.
You know, and they were serving lunches, you know, like soups and all kind of food.
To the-- to the-- to the people-- the workers, you know.
And they also served the-- the German-- the German informants and things like that.
And they didn't have to pay.
So they give them the food.
So that's why they let them do the cooking and everything like that.
And we stayed there for-- let's see, this was November-- till, I think, till January or February.
I think it was February.
And in February they started--
I went back to work in the same thing by laying tracks, you know.
I think the company was Simmons or something-- a German company-- Simmons, you know.
We were laying tracks.
Railroad tracks.
Railroad tracks.

And this in-- about-- in February they started to-- reducing the camp because they might have finished the work and that they were trying to reduce the camp, and they're trying to combine it with another camp which was nearby, which was called Plaszow.
And when they were a reducing the camp, I don't know how, but I was one of the ones that was in the reduction.
And I was sent to Krakow ghetto.
No.
By myself.
They were still-- my two brothers and my sisters remained in Prokocim and I was sent to the Krakow ghetto.
At that point was-- it was bearable.
You-- you did-- you worked a long hard day, but you were able to still have something to eat because you still had x amount of money from-- from-- from home, you know, from Poland.
And the living quarters, I mean, it-- it was survivable.
You know, I mean, you didn't-- you had to-- you didn't have to ask that much.
You had-- I guess there was SS, but it wasn't that-- it was bearable.
It was livable, you know.
It was survivable, you know, something like that.
Yeah, well-- well, my sisters, you know, they-- you know, they had their cooking.
So if you work in a kitchen you always have something to eat.
Schmattas.
It was cold.
You were freezing.
You didn't have the warm coats and everything.
You get up early in the morning, feel like it was cold.
It was raining.
And yeah, it was-- it was miserable.
Yeah.
Then-- then I wound up in ghetto.
In Krakow ghetto.
And in ghetto, I didn't-- I was by myself.
I didn't have anybody.
I don't-- I was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know exactly to-- scrounging for food and-- and a place to sleep in, I don't know-- in a hallway, a corridor.
Hiding out because if they-- if they-- if they caught you, they assembled you to send you out and everything like that.

For about-- this went on for-- in-- you know, just-- it's almost when I watched the movie reminded me being there because being in the-- in the toilets and all these things, and that was me, you know.
Well, my memory was when I-- when it-- when it showed in the movie when it says March 14, when the-- when the SS came over and things like that and took over the ghetto and everything.
It-- it-- it was just like, wow, that's the day I was sent to Auschwitz-- March 14, you know?
And that was almost-- it wasn't the final round-- roundup.
I'm sure there was some people left.
But almost rounded up everybody and that's when I was sent to Auschwitz in-- in open trucks, you know.
On-- on-- in a hallway, in the streets, maybe in some houses.
Because a lot of-- there was houses.
People wasn't living there anymore.
There were houses that people were-- they're empty, you know, because people that lived in those houses were already sent, were in camps.
Were-- there were empty houses and things like that.
That's where you-- wherever you found an open door you lay down and went to sleep.
Yes.
Yes.
I-- I'll be honest with you.
I know I must have eaten-- I must've eaten, otherwise I wouldn't be around here.
But I don't remember where or what or something like that.
Yeah.
It was like wires.
Yeah.

Well, they told you, you know, you're going to be there for awhile and then from there they're gonna send you to different labor camps or different-- not labor camps, like different housings and things like that.
They didn't tell you you're gonna go to Auschwitz or Birkenau or something like that.
But they're gonna resettle you.
I don't know for why.
We were happy where we were, but they were gonna resettle you.
Oh, you see shootings and beatings.
Shootings, everything.
You know, this-- it was just a daily-- daily-- daily fact of life.
Not in Krakow ghetto because this wasn't from my hometown.
I didn't know, but--
Then came March 14, and I was sent to Auschwitz-- actually, Birkenau.
Into Birkenau we went in open trucks.
And-- and again, we were loaded in that truck that you couldn't breath.
You know, just-- in fact, sometimes when they were taking the curve, around the curve I thought we were gonna all fall over and just keel over.
But somehow we made it.
When we got to Birkenau, that-- that's was already all SS.
So they came to the trucks and they used to say to get off the truck.
And some people would say, you stay on the truck, and you get off the truck.
You stay on the truck.
And I noticed the people that they said to get off the truck was more the stronger, more the young-- not the-- not the older-- the younger one.
The-- the-- they ask him to get off.
Me?
They told me to stay on the truck.
But when the guy turn-- turned, I jumped off the truck, you know, and I went with the other one.
And the ones that remained on the truck, I didn't know where they were going.
But I knew-- find out later where they were going.
But then the ones that got off, then they marched us to the camp, and we wound up in-- in-- in camp in a barrack.
You know.
And when we-- after we were there for, I don't know, that evening or that night, they had the people in charge of the barracks-- there were also prisoners, but you know, kapos and things like that.
So I remember one of somebody asking that I came with my father or I came with my mother saying, do you know where they were sent?
So this one guy say, turn around.
You see that chimney over there?
I said, yes.
You see that smoke over there?
He said, maybe you see your mother and father coming out in that smoke.
That's where they are.
They'd been in crematorium.
And I think that's-- that's when I found out, the first night.
You know.
When they were sending you to Auschwitz-Birkenau, had you heard before of Auschwitz and Birkenau?
Not really.
Not really.
No-- I mean, I didn't.
I don't know.
Maybe some people did it.
But no, I-- I didn't.
I didn't.
I didn't hear.
No, not till I-- no, not till we got there.
Not until we got there.
March 14.
Yes, it was only-- it was only a-- by truck a couple hours' ride, you know, from Krakow to--
Yeah, yeah.
It was-- it was cold.
I-- I don't think it was that cold.
Maybe it was early spring.
It wasn't-- I don't remember exactly.
It was-- it was cold.
Because it was still cold the next-- the following weeks while we were on camp.
Yes.
No, no.
Just alone, you know.

So when we got there, and-- which was in March, and at that time, you know, I-- I-- when-- when I saw what's going on, I-- I always thought in my mind I knew that the war has to be-- probably would be over in about another five, six weeks.
It's-- was close to Passover, that by Passover the war's going to be over.
And since-- if my sisters, my brother, if they find out I was sent to Auschwitz, so they probably later on know for sure that I'm not going to be alive.
And I'm gonna surprise everybody.
I'm gonna fight, and I'm gonna make it.
I'm gonna be alive.
I said, I don't care if I don't eat for six weeks or if I don't do nothing.
I'm going to somehow manage to stay alive.
And when they-- when they gave you the-- they give you food, like rations.
It was like called a soup.
I don't know what it was.
They brought it in a barrel, and they give it to you.
Basically a lot of times, if they had some left over, they give some to the younger.
When I was there, I think I was the youngest one in the group at that time.
So they would give me another portion.

And then another thing what I did is when they emptied the food, when they emptied the barrels, they had to take them back to the-- like the mess hall, which was, I don't know, four, five barracks or something like that.
So I always jumped out, and I'm going to carry the barrels back to the thing.
So when you--
To the mess hall.
So we carried the barrels back because there was some little food left in the barrels.
So you just use your finger and took it out.
You were able to eat.
Sometimes when you got to the mess hall you found another barrel with some food or something.
So I did that.
And at night and-- when they give you the portions, you finished everything.
You didn't leave over because you didn't leave for anybody to steal it from you or take it away from you.
Yeah, yeah, yeah.
I was given a number, a tattoo, yeah.
Yes, it's 108262.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah, I was tattooed.
No.
No, no.
Just the needle.
Just the needle.
I don't know whether they did anything or not but they just-- you know.
This was in-- in-- that-- when I was there in that camp, that was in-- not the main camp.
That was a camp that was just like-- I don't even know exactly what barrack I was in that camp.
When we finally got to the new camp I was, I think, barrack 14.
But this camp, I-- I don't remember what the name of the--
Birkenau, yes.
Yeah, because at that time, you know, they had-- this was the old camp.
And then also in Birkenau, they moved us to a new camp, which was a little bigger barracks and things a little differently than this one.
The conditions there was mud.
The bathrooms was like a big hole in the-- you know, like a-- like a big-- big hole.
A rail to hold onto something when you want to go to the bathroom.
It was-- you-- you heard of cold, freezing, not sanitary at all.
And I was working in the-- on this-- with this-- in this company while I was working.
They were doing some laying water pipes.
We were building-- building a new camp.
They were doing pipes, you know.
We were  digging ditches to build the pipes.
So this one kapo, which was called, and he was German political prisoner.
And he kind of-- kind of liked me.
So I was polishing his shoes.
And when I was working in the camp I didn't do that physical work like some of the other ones.
So he asked me, hey, Sam, have you had your typhoid fever yet?
I says, no.
He says, well, you'll get it.
I said, well, why do I have to get it?
Believe me, you'll get it.
And sure enough about a week or two weeks later, I wound up with the typhoid fever.
So when I had the typhoid fever, I-- I couldn't stand up.
So I was sent to the dispensary.
It was called like a hospital, or so it called.
And they had-- the-- the barracks was like in like-- in like-- something like I think inside here.
You know, you sleep one in the top.
It was about four-- four in one of these bunks and four in-- four in the middle.
They sometimes seemed they  had them on the bottom if they had-- if they needed to.
But basically it was in the middle and the top.
And I was on the top.
And I remember-- I had a high fever-- but I remember I couldn't eat the bread, but I was very thirsty.
And I was eating the-- drinking the soup.
And there was this other one was also in sick.
But evidently, he wasn't-- whatever his sickness, wasn't like mine.
He could eat.
So I gave him my bread, and he gave me some-- half of his soup.
You know.
And I don't know exactly for how long I was.
And then I was-- And he used to also even help me to go down to the bathroom because I had diarrhea.
And then he took me, and we went back.
And he just fell asleep, and I had to go again.
And I didn't want to wake him.
So I tried to go myself.
So when I went-- tried to go down but I fell.
And now-- and they had-- wanted to clean the floor, or whatever it was.
There was water on the floor.
I don't remember exactly how long I lay on the floor.
But I had a shirt on.
That's all I had on, a shirt.
And when I woke up the shirt was wet and everything.
And I took my shirt off.
I had a blanket.
I remember I had a blanket.
I was-- didn't share a blanket with somebody.
So I went to the bathroom.
I threw my shirt away.
And I had just a blanket.
And I didn't get any medication, no food, no nothing.
But somehow after, I don't know, three days, four days, or five days, my fever broke and they-- and somebody-- orderly came along and asked me.
And they-- and they gave me a-- a spoon-- tablespoon.
I don't know, it's like chalk.
It's supposed to stop for the diarrhea.
And I wanted to get out of there, because I know if you stay there, they're gonna round you up and send you to the gas chamber.
So the next day, I went in front of the-- whoever that releases you.
I says, I want to go out, and I want to get back to my, you know, to the work detail.
So he gave me a-- like with the finger her gave me a push and I fell over.
[LAUGHS] You know.
So the next day I went back and I says, I want to get out.
He said, well, how are you gonna be working-- how can you work in your work detail?

I said, I'll explain to him the-- who-- the company, I'm-- the work detail I am, and who that guy is-- my kapo and everything-- and he would probably-- you know, wouldn't have to work, do physical work and everything.
He said-- so he discharged me.
He let me get out.
But that day, I remember there was like a hole in front of my eyes.
Like-- like when I walked, like-- like a ditch.
I don't know.
I mean, I just-- it was weird.
I-- I couldn't see.
And even so, that day, because in the daytime, you weren't supposed to be in camp.
You were supposed to be out on the labor.
So I had to hide out that day until the guys came back, you know, till the guys came back to the barracks.
So I-- the next day I finally went back to work.
And-- and that-- and that was in the-- you know, I went back to work and I got back on my feet a little bit over there.
Oh, I don't know.
I hid behind the barrack.
I hid in the bathroom.
You know, I went in the bathroom.
I mean, I don't know, if I saw somebody here, I went there.
If I saw them in the front of the barrack I went behind the barrack.
I pretended I was doing something like-- like-- like I wasn't lost in the thing, like I was do-- like I had a shovel like doing a ditch or something.
Anything not to look suspicious, like you know, you not-- you don't belong in here.
Yeah, he-- his name was Marmalade [?
Franz. ?] I don't know why they called him Marmalade [?
Franz. ?] But he was-- he was one of the political prisoner.
And he was-- he was German.
And he was there for a long time.
And everybody knew him, you know.
And he-- he helped me.
He helped me a lot.
If it wouldn't be for him I probably wouldn't be here.
You know.
And then-- and then-- and then was one incidence when I was there.
I don't know.
I don't know-- I never-- we were working, you know, in the-- in the-- not-- you know, there was the immediate camp was the barracks, which were enclosed with barbed wire and everything.
But when you were working, you were working on the outskirt of the camps.
And they had out-- they had outposts, you know, like-- and they had with submachine guns on the outlooks and posts and things like that.
And we were working on the outskirts.
And that was close to the German-- the German barracks and things like this.
And I don't remember-- one time I remember-- I don't know why or something-- I was looking-- I was near the SS barracks and I was looking for some food.
And I got caught.
And they got me in the barracks.
And I had to lay down on chairs.
And they hit me with a stick.
And the stick broke.
And by the time they wanted to get another stick, I jumped up and ran away.
But they didn't chase me.
So they-- whatever they did, they let me go.
You know.
Well, they could shoot you.
They could shoot you.
I mean-- I mean, like I say, they just-- evidently they felt I didn't do such a bad thing.
So they didn't shoot me.
You know.
You know, while I was there, and-- the conditions in there-- you got up at 4:00, 5 o'clock in the morning for appel.
You stood at attention.
Sometimes somebody was missing so you had to stay for hours in attention until they found him.
And it wouldn't-- it couldn't be-- it wasn't that he was missing, tried to escape.
He could be dead and didn't show up for the appel.
Because a lot of people over there at that particular time, they wish they were dead.
You weren't a human.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We were there, and I don't know.
I guess you want to live-- you know, I don't know.
Many, many a people killed themselves by going to the barbed wire.
Yes.
And-- a couple-- one time they had-- I think a couple prisoners were trying to escape, and they were caught.
And they had a public hanging.
And they made-- they made everyone line-- everybody get and line up in this place and watch him being hanged.
Yeah.
Watched him be hanged.
So-- so to tell you what-- to tell you what's going to happen to you if you tried to escape.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] They-- they didn't need any reason to-- to hit you or to beat you or to do things like that.
They didn't need any reason.
I was-- I know-- I don't know.
I broke out one time in a-- with a rash all over me.
I didn't have anything to-- to-- to clean it or everything.
There was no medication or nothing.
I don't know for how many days it lasted.
But somehow it cleared up, and I-- and I survived.
One day I had under my arm-- I had a big boil.
And I went to the dispensary, and they looked at it.
And they said, well, come back tomorrow and we'll-- we'll drain it or something like that.
So I went back the next day, and I'll tell you.
[LAUGHS]
